meta.title: E87 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 1 Series E87 engine — N45, N46, N43, N52, M47, N47. Real UK costs, the M47/N47 diesel switch explained, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series E87 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E87 engine typically costs £1,200–£4,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the 1.6-litre petrols sit at £1,200–£2,500, the 2.0-litre petrols at £1,800–£3,200, the 3.0 straight-six at £2,500–£4,500, and the N47 diesel at £2,200–£4,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,040. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 1 Series E87 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 straight-six (130i) is the most reliable E87 engine — naturally aspirated, smooth, and free of the N47's timing chain risk and the N43's injector woes. Among diesels, the M47D20 (pre‑LCI 118d/120d) is more robust than the N47 that replaced it.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series E87?

acceptedAnswer.text: Common issues include: timing chain stretch on the N47 diesel; endless injector, coil and NOx sensor failures on the N43 petrol; VANOS solenoid wear on the N46 and N52 engines; and — unrelated to the engine — rear subframe mount cracking on high‑mileage cars.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series E87 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and which side of the March 2007 facelift your car sits on. Pre‑LCI buyers get the more robust M47 diesel and N46 petrol; post‑LCI buyers get the improved but chain‑affected N47 diesel and the troublesome N43 petrol. The N52 straight‑six is the most reliable option regardless of facelift.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW E87 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don't recommend outright avoidance — but a post‑LCI N47 diesel without a documented timing‑chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected N43 petrol without injector history is the second. The 123d's twin‑turbo adds even more complexity and cost.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E87 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: N47/N43 — cold‑start rattle or rough running; N43 — check engine light, misfires, or injector codes; N46/N52 — VANOS fault codes or rough idle. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 1 Series E87 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £7,000 130i, a £4,500 recon is 64% — just inside our 70% BMW threshold. Against a tired £3,500 E87, the same recon is 129% — walk away. Value the car first.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW E87 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 straight‑six can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The N46 petrol is similarly durable. The N47 and N43's limiting factors are the timing chain (N47) and injector/NOx sensor issues (N43) — addressed proactively, either can reach 150,000+ miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E87, E81, E82 and E88?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the first‑generation 1 Series platform and most engines. The E87 is the 5‑door hatchback (2004–2011, this page), the E81 is the 3‑door hatchback (2007–2011), the E82 is the 2‑door coupé (2007–2013), and the E88 is the 2‑door convertible (2007–2013). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 1 Series E87 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most units. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date (pre‑ or post‑March 2007) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 1 Series E87 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E87 five-door hatchback — from the 1.6-litre four-cylinder petrols to the 3.0-litre straight-six and the N47 diesel that defined this generation. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the timing chain and injector issues that shape the E87's ownership story. Backed by 240+ real UK E87 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].
hero.dataIntegrityNote: 240 matches the EM_Proprietary_Data_2025 figure for BMW 1 Series E87 exactly. Production years verified via Wikipedia: September 2004 to June 2011 (LCI from 2007).

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series E87 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E87 five-door hatchback specifically — including the pre- and post-facelift engine changes that split this generation in two.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are provisional — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E87 engine family. The N47 diesel carries the highest risk due to well-documented timing chain stretch and tensioner failure. The N43 petrols suffer from endless coil, injector and NOx sensor issues. The N52 straight-six is the most robust of the E87 engine lineup. The M47 is considered more reliable than its N47 successor.

dataCorrections: Correction applied (addition): The E87's engine lineup comprises seven distinct variants across petrol and diesel. The 116i used the N45B16A (115 bhp) pre-facelift and N43B16 (122 bhp) post-facelift.
Correction applied (addition): The 118i and 120i used the N46B20 pre-facelift (129–150 bhp) and N43B20 post-facelift (143–170 bhp).
Correction applied (addition): The 130i used the N52B30 straight-six producing 258–265 bhp.
Correction applied (addition): Diesel engines: M47D20 (122–163 bhp) pre-facelift replaced by N47D20 (115–204 bhp) from March 2007.
Correction applied: The 123d used a twin-turbo variant of the N47 producing 204 bhp.
Correction applied: The E87 was produced from September 2004 to June 2011. A facelift (LCI) in March 2007 brought significant engine changes.

overview.intro: The E87 is the five-door hatchback variant of the first-generation BMW 1 Series, launched in September 2004 as BMW's entry-level model. It was the first compact hatchback from BMW to feature rear-wheel drive — a layout that would define the 1 Series until the F40 generation. The engine lineup spanned 1.6 and 2.0-litre four-cylinder petrols, a 3.0-litre straight-six petrol in the 130i, and 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesels. A significant March 2007 facelift (LCI) brought revised styling, new engines (N43 petrols and N47 diesels), and improved fuel economy across the range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L2 platform • Predecessor: BMW 3 Series Compact • Successor: BMW 1 Series F20 • Sibling body styles in this generation: E81 (3-door hatchback), E82 (coupé), E88 (convertible).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E87 generated 240 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with the N47 diesel variants accounting for the largest share of demand.

items.quote: The M47 is the last of BMW's more robust diesel fours — no rear-mounted timing chain to worry about, and a documented reputation for durability when serviced properly. The 163 bhp 120d is the pick of the bunch.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want E87 diesel torque without the N47's timing chain anxiety.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre straight-six is the E87's sweetheart — smooth, naturally aspirated, and free of the N43's injector and NOx sensor woes. It's the most reliable engine in the E87 lineup.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who want the ultimate E87 driving experience.

items.quote: The rear-mounted timing chain is this generation's defining risk — cold-start rattle is the warning, and a snapped chain means full replacement. The 123d's twin-turbo adds even more complexity.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented chain-replacement history and a healthy budget.

items.quote: A snapped timing chain on the N47 means piston-to-valve contact and a full engine replacement. The rear-mounted design makes access difficult and labour-intensive.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who can afford a £4,500+ replacement if the chain lets go.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre N46 petrol offers a good balance of performance and running costs, with fewer horror stories than the N43 that replaced it. Valvetronic and VANOS are its only real wear items.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the N43's injector issues.

items.quote: The 130i is often forgotten in the 1 Series conversation, but it's the E87's hidden gem — 265 bhp, a glorious straight-six soundtrack, and the most reliable engine in the range.
items.whoItsFor: enthusiasts who want a modern BMW straight-six before they all went turbo.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Budget Choice — cheap to fix, but N43 has known injector problems.
Solid Choice — N46 is reliable; N43 has injector and NOx sensor issues.
Best Petrol — the E87's most dependable engine.
Best Diesel — the more robust diesel option.
High Risk — budget for chain work; 123d adds twin-turbo complexity.

economicsRule.title: The E87 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500 N47 recon against a £5,000 E87 sits at 90% — brushing our 70% BMW threshold. On a sub-£4,000 E87, that recon is simply not viable. Against a well-kept £7,000+ 130i, the same recon drops to a more palatable figure — but the N47 is never cheap to replace. Value the car first, then the repair.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E87 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N46B20 (118i/120i) — cheap parts, no turbo, no timing chain nightmares.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E87 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N47D20 (123d) — timing chain work and twin-turbo complexity add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E87 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M47D20 (pre-LCI 120d) — diesel torque without the N47's timing chain risk.

problems.title: N47 Diesel Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The N47 diesel's rear-mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time. Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning. The 123d's twin-turbo setup adds complexity and cost.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on an N47 E87, get the chain inspected immediately — an £800–£2,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,500–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E87 worth under £4,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays viable.

cta.label: Compare N47 engine prices for your E87 →

problems.title: N43 Petrol Injector and NOx Sensor Failure
problems.rootCause: The N43 petrol engines suffer from endless coil, injector and NOx sensor issues. They run hot for emissions purposes, which cooks rubber gaskets and plastic timing chain guides. High-pressure fuel pump failure is also common.
problems.recommendation: If your N43 E87 develops rough running or a check engine light, have the injectors and NOx sensors diagnosed immediately. A £400–£1,000 repair is manageable, but these engines can become money pits — be realistic about the car's value.

cta.label: Compare N43 engine prices for your E87 →

problems.title: N46/N52 VANOS Solenoid and Oil Leak Issues
problems.rootCause: The N46 and N52 engines suffer from VANOS solenoid wear, leading to rough idle and loss of low-end power. Oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing are also common.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough idle or a VANOS fault code, have the solenoids inspected and cleaned or replaced — a £300–£700 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N46/N52 engine prices for your E87 →

problems.title: E87 Rear Subframe Mount Cracking
problems.affectedModels: All E87 variants, particularly high-mileage examples.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non-engine enquiry on this generation — the rear axle carrier panel can crack around the subframe mounting points under sustained load, a known issue shared with the E90 3 Series.

tieredCosts.work: Reinforcement plate fitting before cracking starts

problems.recommendation: Have this checked on any E87 over 80,000 miles — it's the single most common reason an otherwise sound E87 fails an inspection.

replacementCosts.h2: E87 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — E87 shares identical engine-bay access with its E81/E82/E88 siblings, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E87 five-door hatchback only. E81 (3-door hatchback), E82 (coupé) and E88 (convertible) share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.

mostCommonFailures: 2. N43 injector and NOx sensor failure
